content:
Programming

Web Development

Authentication

Cybersecurity

JavaScript

23 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A beginner-to-clarity walkthrough of how modern authentication actually works, told through the misconceptions I had to unlearn.

I have used hundreds of apps. Instagram, Gmail, GitHub, my banking app. And I kept noticing the same thing:
I never log in.
I open Instagram > feed loads. I open Gmail > inbox loads. I haven’t typed those passwords in months. But my banking app? It kicks me out if I look away for five minutes.
I knew the words. “Access token.” “Refresh token.” “JWT.” I could put them on my resume. But I couldn’t explain why one app remembers me for a year and another forgets me before I finish my coffee(Being a Full Stack Engineer LOL).
So I sat down and worked through it properly, asking every basic question I was too embarrassed to ask, and getting corrected each time I was wrong.
This article is that whole journey. Every misconception I had is a section here, because I’m willing to bet you have at least two of them.
Let’s start where every developer starts.

The starting point: what you already know
You’ve built signup and login. It looks like this:
SIGNUP
User submits: email + password
You: hash the password, INSERT into users ┌────┬──────────────────┬─────────────────────┐
│ id │ email │ password_hash │
├────┼──────────────────┼─────────────────────┤
│ 42 │ hannan@gmail.com │ $2b$10$xK9... │
└────┴──────────────────┴─────────────────────┘LOGIN
User submits: email + password
You: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?
bcrypt.compare(password, row.password_hash)
✅ match → "Login successful"Clean. Works. Ship it.
Now here’s the question that nobody answered for me properly:
Login succeeded. You send back “Login successful”. The request ends. The user clicks “My Profile”. How does your server know it’s Hannan?
It doesn’t.

The real problem: HTTP has amnesia
Every HTTP request is a stranger knocking on your door. Your server has zero memory of the previous request.
Request 1: POST /login → "I'm Hannan, here's my password" ✅
Request 2: GET /my-profile → "..."Server: "Who are you?"
Browser: "Uhh... I logged in like 2 seconds ago?"
Server: "Never seen you before in my life."There is no invisible thread connecting request 1 and request 2. Your server genuinely cannot tell whether GET /my-profile came from Hannan, from an attacker, or from a bot.
So every authentication system ever built exists to answer one single question:
“How does request #2 prove it’s the same person from request #1 — without sending the password again?”
That’s it. Sessions, JWTs, refresh tokens, OAuth — all of them are just different answers to that one sentence.
Burn that in, because everything below is a variation on it.

Why not just resend the password?
The obvious idea:
GET /my-profile
email: hannan@gmail.com
password: mypass123It works. It’s also terrible:

• ❌ The password crosses the network 50 times a day instead of once → 50 chances to leak

• ❌ The browser has to store the raw password to keep resending it

• ❌ You run bcrypt on every request — bcrypt is deliberately slow (~100ms). Your API is now dead

• ❌ You can’t log out. Can’t expire. Can’t ban a device.
So we need something that proves identity without being the password.
We need a ticket.

Answer #1: Sessions, the server remembers you
Think of a cinema. You show your ID once at the counter. They hand you ticket #A7X9. That ticket isn’t your ID — it’s a meaningless random number. But the cinema wrote in their notebook: A7X9 = Hannan.
Now you just flash the ticket at every door.
Your database gets a second table:
CREATE TABLE sessions (
id VARCHAR(64) PRIMARY KEY, -- "a7x9k2mp..." random string
user_id INT, -- 42 → links to users.id
expires_at TIMESTAMP,
created_at TIMESTAMP
);That user_id foreign key is the entire trick. It's the physical link between "who logged in" and "who is making this request."
Login:
// 1. what you already do
const user = await db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?", [email]);
if (!await bcrypt.compare(password, user.password_hash))
return res.status(401).send("Wrong password");// 2. NEW — create the ticket
const sessionId = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");
await db.query(
"INSERT INTO sessions (id, user_id, expires_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
[sessionId, user.id, Date.now() + 7*24*60*60*1000]
);// 3. hand the ticket to the browser
res.cookie("sid", sessionId, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: "lax" });Every request after that:
const sid = req.cookies.sid; // browser sent it automatically
const session = await db.query(
"SELECT * FROM sessions WHERE id = ? AND expires_at > NOW()", [sid]
);
if (!session) return res.status(401).send("Session invalid");const user = await db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", [session.user_id]);
// 🎉 Server now knows: this is Hannan, id 42You never asked for the password again. You went cookie → sessions table → user_id → users table.
And logout is beautiful:
await db.query("DELETE FROM sessions WHERE id = ?", [sid]);Ticket destroyed. Even if an attacker copied that cookie, it’s now a dead number. Instant revocation.
Honestly? For a normal website, you could stop reading here. Sessions are underrated.
But two pains show up at scale.

Where sessions start hurting
Pain 1 — A database hit on every single request. Load a page that fires 30 API calls → 30 SELECT queries against your sessions table. At 10,000 users, fine. At 10 million, that's a very hot table. (Fix: move it to Redis. Most companies do exactly this and never think about it again.)
Pain 2 — Multiple servers.
┌── Server A (created session A7X9 in its memory)
Load Balancer┤
└── Server B ("A7X9? never heard of it") ❌Fixable with shared Redis — but now every service, every region, every microservice needs access to that shared store.
Which led someone to ask: what if the ticket could describe itself, so we never need to look it up?

Answer #2: JWT — the server forgets, the ticket carries everything
Instead of a meaningless A7X9, give the user a signed ID card:
{ "userId": 42, "role": "user", "exp": 1721650000 } ← readable
+ SIGNATURE (made with your server's secret key) ← unforgeableNo sessions table. No lookup.
// LOGIN
const token = jwt.sign({ userId: user.id }, process.env.SECRET, { expiresIn: "15m" });// EVERY REQUEST AFTER
const payload = jwt.verify(token, process.env.SECRET);
// payload.userId === 42 → done. ZERO database queries. ⚡That verify step is pure math on the CPU — microseconds. It scales beautifully.
⚠️ Important security fact: a JWT payload is Base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone can read it. The signature proves it wasn’t tampered with — it does not hide anything. Never put secrets inside a JWT.
But now you have a monster problem
You cannot log the user out.
There’s no row to delete. The token is out in the world, valid, signed by you. Your server has no memory that it exists.
User: "Someone stole my laptop! Log me out!"
You: "I... can't. Your token works for 15 more minutes.
There's nothing in my database to delete."Same for a banned user, a fired employee, a changed password, a revoked admin role. The token doesn’t care.
So you’re trapped:
Expiry Result 30 days ✅ great UX Stolen token = 30 days of free access 💀 15 min ✅ safe User retypes their password every 15 minutes 🤬
Both are unacceptable. This exact deadlock is why refresh tokens were invented.

Answer #3: Refresh tokens, use two tokens instead of one
The insight is almost embarrassingly simple:
One token that’s fast but short-lived. One token that’s checked in the DB but rarely used.
Access Token Refresh Token Lives 15 minutes 30 days Sent to every API endpoint only /auth/refresh Type JWT (self-contained) opaque random string Stored in DB? ❌ no ✅ yes Can be killed? no (but dies in 15 min) yes — revoke the row
Read that table twice, because it is the answer:

• The access token gives you JWT’s speed, no DB hit on your 30 API calls per page.

• The refresh token gives you the session’s kill switch, because it basically is a session row.
You get speed and control. The only cost: revocation isn’t instant, it’s within 15 minutes.
Your database, final form
-- unchanged
CREATE TABLE users (
id INT PRIMARY KEY,
email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE,
password_hash VARCHAR(255)
);-- this is just the sessions table, renamed 🙂
CREATE TABLE refresh_tokens (
id VARCHAR(64) PRIMARY KEY, -- SHA-256 hash of the token
user_id INT REFERENCES users(id), -- 👈 THE LINK
expires_at TIMESTAMP,
revoked BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
device_info VARCHAR(255), -- "Chrome / Windows"
created_at TIMESTAMP
);Look closely. It’s the sessions table with a new name. That’s the punchline nobody tells beginners:
A refresh token is a session that you only check every 15 minutes instead of on every request.

Now the 10 questions I actually got wrong
Everything above is the clean version. Here’s the messy version: the actual misconceptions I had, and what corrected them. If you’ve read tutorials and still feel fuzzy, your fuzziness is probably in this list.

❓ Q1: “Why does the server need a refresh_tokens table, but not an access_tokens table?"
My guess: “So it knows which user the token belongs to.”
Wrong — and this one’s subtle. The access token already proves who you are:
jwt.verify(accessToken, SECRET) → { userId: 42 }Identity, proven, with zero DB access. So “knowing who it belongs to” can’t be the reason — the access token pulls that off without a table.
The real answer: the DB row is the server’s ability to say no.
Question Who answers it “Who are you?” The signature — math, no DB “Are you still allowed?” The DB row — only a database can answer this
A signature can prove identity forever. It can never tell you the user was banned five minutes ago, clicked logout, or changed their password. Math doesn’t know about events. Only a database does.
Access token = identity. Refresh token row = permission.
And why no access_tokens table? Two reasons, and you need both:

• You don’t need to kill it — it kills itself in 15 minutes

• You can’t afford to check it — that’s a DB hit on every request
Token used 500×/day → no DB. Dies on its own. (access)
Token used ~96×/day → full DB check every time. (refresh)You put the expensive security check on the rare action, not the common one. That’s the whole trick.

❓ Q2: “The refresh token is the short-lived one, right?”
Backwards. I had the lifetimes flipped, and this breaks everything downstream.
Token Lifetime Access token 15 minutes ⏱️ SHORT Refresh token 30 days 📅 LONG
Why this way round? Ask which token is more dangerous if stolen.
The access token goes to every endpoint — /profile, /orders, /cart. It's constantly in flight, in headers, in JS memory. High exposure → so make it die fast. Stolen? The attacker gets 15 minutes.
The refresh token sits in an httpOnly cookie, JavaScript can't read it, and it goes to exactly one endpoint. Low exposure → so it's safe to let it live long. And if it is stolen, you can revoke the row.
Short life protects the exposed token. A DB row protects the hidden one. Each is defended by the tool that fits it.
9:00 AM Login (password → DB ✅)
├── access token → dies 9:15 AM
└── refresh token → dies in 30 days9:15 AM access expired → silently refreshed
9:30 AM access expired → silently refreshed
...
6:00 PM still browsing, never typed the password again🎟️ Access token = the ticket that keeps expiring. 🪪 Refresh token = the ID card that keeps getting you new tickets.
You wouldn’t want your ID card expiring every 15 minutes.

❓ Q3: “Is a refresh token like when my browser autofills my password?”
No — and this confusion is extremely common. They’re different layers entirely.
Browser autofill:

• Lives in Chrome, on your laptop

• Your server has no idea it exists

• It literally stores your password and types it for you

• You still submit POST /login, still hit bcrypt, still do the full login
Refresh token:

• You never see a login page at all
With autofill
